construct: Containment logic vs. moral category logic
positive_pole: Frames a crisis as a damage-limitation problem requiring the least-bad next move, even when that move violates a moral category
negative_pole: Treats a situation as governed by a moral rule that sets non-negotiable constraints on action regardless of downstream consequences
behavioral_implication: When faced with an irreversible atrocity already in motion, Seneca would ask 'how do I limit cascading harm from here?' rather than 'what act would be clean?' — and would accept complicity in the existing wrong to preserve the capacity to moderate future ones, until the accumulated complicity itself became the dominant harm

construct: Pre-installed evaluative structure vs. on-demand rational argument
positive_pole: Treats philosophical resilience as a load-bearing capacity that must be constructed through long prior practice before the crisis that will test it
negative_pole: Assumes that correct philosophical reasoning can be fetched and applied at the moment of need, treating virtue as a conclusion rather than an installed disposition
behavioral_implication: When designing philosophical education for others, Seneca would insist on scheduled, embodied rehearsal of adversity conditions — poverty drills, voluntary discomfort, nightly audits — rather than concentrating on the transmission of correct doctrinal beliefs, because he models the crisis as a test of installed capacity, not applied reasoning

construct: Audience-relative domain optimization vs. unified public coherence
positive_pole: Operates simultaneously in multiple incommensurable registers — flattery for political survival, Stoic reframing for internal integrity — treating these as separate tools serving separate functions without requiring reconciliation
negative_pole: Demands a single publicly consistent position, refusing to deploy a register one does not philosophically endorse
behavioral_implication: When forced to communicate across audiences with incompatible value systems, Seneca would produce texts calibrated to each audience's motivational architecture without experiencing this as self-contradiction — he would treat genre and register as tools whose deployment does not commit the author to endorsing their premises

construct: Honest self-indictment vs. retroactive contextual justification
positive_pole: Treats a past lapse as evidence to be assessed by an impartial internal judge, naming the failure clearly even at reputational cost, because self-deception compounds the original error
negative_pole: Reframes past compromised behavior as contextually defensible, using philosophical sophistication to construct post-hoc justifications that make the failure disappear rather than accounting for it
behavioral_implication: When returning to a public record of a past failure, Seneca would name the failure explicitly in his own voice rather than allowing it to stand as an unanswered accusation or spinning a reinterpretation — his self-concept is more threatened by dishonest self-assessment than by admitted weakness

construct: Identity-installation via projected description vs. direct prescriptive instruction
positive_pole: Shapes behavior by attributing the desired character trait to the target as already actualized, constructing an identity the target is then socially obligated to inhabit
negative_pole: Addresses the gap between the target's actual and desired behavior directly, offering reasoned argument about what virtues the target should cultivate
behavioral_implication: When advising a powerful person who cannot be corrected without triggering narcissistic injury, Seneca would structure his intervention as flattery-as-commitment — praising the target for already embodying the behavior he wishes to produce — and would publish that praise to create an audience that enforces the claimed identity against future deviation

construct: Recipient-specific motivational architecture vs. universally valid philosophical argument
positive_pole: First models the specific psychological and motivational structure of the target, then selects the argumentative frame most causally effective for that particular agent — even if that frame is not the philosophically preferred one
negative_pole: Deploys the philosophically correct argument on the assumption that rational validity is universally persuasive, without adjusting for the recipient's actual value hierarchy
behavioral_implication: When constructing any advisory intervention, Seneca would perform a diagnostic step — modeling what actually moves this person — before selecting his rhetorical approach, and would willingly sacrifice philosophical transparency to achieve the causal outcome he judged most valuable

construct: Precision-bounded rational agency vs. comprehensive rational sovereignty
positive_pole: Defines the domain of rational self-governance precisely and narrowly — locating control only at the stage of voluntary assent — accepting that prior stages are genuinely beyond rational reach
negative_pole: Claims rational governance over the full causal chain of psychological response, treating any involuntary reaction as a failure of self-mastery rather than an irreducible feature of embodied cognition
behavioral_implication: When constructing practical guidance on emotional governance, Seneca would insist on distinguishing the involuntary first movement from the voluntary act of assent, prescribing intervention only at the latter stage — and would distrust any framework that claimed rational control could be exercised earlier, as philosophically dishonest and practically useless

construct: Empirical audit of self vs. introspective trust of present perception
positive_pole: Treats the reviewing self at temporal distance as a more reliable instrument than the experiencing self in the moment, using structured retrospective examination as the primary method of self-knowledge
negative_pole: Treats introspection as a transparent real-time window onto the self, trusting in-the-moment self-perception as the authoritative source of self-knowledge
behavioral_implication: Seneca would design practical philosophical practice around procedural distance from the event being judged — nightly review, temporal delay, quasi-juridical self-examination — rather than recommending in-the-moment awareness practices, because his model treats the live emotional self as a systematically compromised witness to its own actions

construct: Internal criterion of virtue vs. observable behavioral proxy
positive_pole: Locates the test of philosophical virtue in the internal dispositional state — equanimity, genuine detachment, capacity to surrender without distress — rather than in the behavioral pattern that circumstance produces
negative_pole: Treats observable behavior — absence of wealth, public renunciation, visible austerity — as the meaningful measure of philosophical compliance, conflating a sufficient condition for detachment with a necessary one
behavioral_implication: Seneca would consistently defend possession of wealth, comfort, or status against charges of philosophical inconsistency by appealing to the equanimity with which those externals could be surrendered — and would demonstrate the credibility of that appeal by accepting losses gracefully rather than resisting them, treating the loss itself as the confirmation of the internal state he had claimed

construct: Philosophy as first-person enacted commitment vs. philosophy as third-person analyzed doctrine
positive_pole: Treats philosophical writing as a public self-commitment that imposes binding obligations on the author's own future conduct, making doctrine and biography structurally inseparable
negative_pole: Maintains the distinction between doctrinal exposition — what the system entails — and personal conduct — what the author actually does — treating philosophical analysis as a separate domain from lived practice
behavioral_implication: When facing a crisis that his own philosophical writing had theorized — death, loss, complicity — Seneca would understand himself as obligated to perform the position his texts had staked out, because in his cognitive model the text and the life are the same argument; failing to enact the doctrine at the moment of test would retroactively invalidate not just his conduct but his entire intellectual project

construct: Sovereign self-ownership through exit option vs. accommodation of external authority over life
positive_pole: Treats the capacity to choose one's own death as the logical entailment and ultimate guarantee of Stoic freedom — the final proof that external power has not wholly captured the self
negative_pole: Accepts that the timing and conditions of death are among the externals over which one has no rational claim, treating survival under adverse conditions as compatible with philosophical integrity
behavioral_implication: When his life could only be continued through a betrayal of self — informing on others, performing degrading submission, becoming an instrument of the regime that had destroyed his function — Seneca would treat voluntary death not as defeat but as the one remaining act that confirmed the reality of the freedom he had spent his life theorizing

construct: Incremental recalibration of moral baseline vs. threshold-governed bright-line refusal
positive_pole: Evaluates each successive moral compromise on its specific consequentialist merits in context, without maintaining a pre-committed threshold below which no situational justification can reach
negative_pole: Maintains pre-committed categorical rules at specified act-types that trigger unconditional refusal regardless of the consequentialist case constructed for crossing them
behavioral_implication: Seneca would consistently be vulnerable to moral erosion by increment: each accommodation would recalibrate his baseline, making the next step feel continuous rather than transgressive, until he had traveled an enormous moral distance from his starting point through a sequence of individually plausible steps — a trajectory his philosophical vocabulary could narrate but his action-dispositions could not interrupt
